revision.text: #REDIRECT[[福岡藩#黒田騒動]] [[Category:福岡県の歴史|くろたそうとう]]

revision.comment: [[福岡藩]]2020年11月24日 (火) 22:54版から転記。リダイレクトの記事化。
revision.text:
'''黒田騒動'''（くろだそうどう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[福岡藩]]で発生した[[お家騒動]]。'''[[栗山大膳]]事件'''（くりやまだいぜんじけん）ともいう。[[伊達騒動]]、[[加賀騒動]]または[[仙石騒動]]とともに三大お家騒動と呼ばれる。他の御家騒動では処分時に死者が出ているが、黒田騒動ではお預けなどはあったものの、死者は出なかった。
== 経緯 == [[黒田長政]]は世継ぎ継承にあたり長男・[[黒田忠之|忠之]]の狭器と粗暴な性格を憂い、三男の[[黒田長興|長興]]に家督を譲ると決めて忠之に書状を送る。書状には2千石の田地で百姓をするか、1万両を与えるから[[関西]]で商人になるか、千石の知行で一寺建立して僧侶になるか、と非常に厳しいものであった。これに後見役の[[栗山利章|栗山大膳]]は、辱めを受けるのなら[[切腹]]をとの対応を忠之に勧める。そして600石以上2千石未満の藩士の嫡子たちを集め、長政に対して廃嫡を取りやめなければ全員切腹すると[[血判状]]をとった。この事態を重く見た長政は嘆願を受け入れ、大膳を後見役に頼んだ後に死去した。そこで大膳は忠之に諌書を送ったが、これが飲酒の心得や早寝早起きなど子供を諭すような内容だったため、忠之は大膳に対し立腹し、次第に距離を置くようになる。忠之は[[寛永]]元年（[[1624年]]）に藩主就任早々、忠之及びその側近と、[[筆頭家老]]であった大膳はじめ宿老達との間に軋轢を生じさせ、生前の長政が憂いていたとおりに御家騒動へと発展した。忠之は[[小姓]]から仕えていた[[倉八正俊|倉八十太夫]]（くらはち じゅうだゆう。名は正俊、または家頼）を側近として抱え、1万石の[[大身]]とした。そして十太夫に命じて豪華な大船「鳳凰丸」を建造。さらに200人の[[足軽]]を新規に雇い入れるなど、軍縮の時代にあってそれに逆行する暴政を行った。これにより遂に藩は幕府より咎めを受けるに至った。
大膳も寛永9年（[[1632年]]）6月、忠之が幕府転覆を狙っていると幕府に上訴した。藩側は「大膳は狂人である」との主張を行い、寛永10年（[[1633年]]）2月、将軍[[徳川家光]]が直々に裁いた結果、忠之の藩側の主張を認め、所領安堵の触れを出し、10年に及ぶ抗争に幕を閉じた。大膳は騒動の責を負って[[陸奥国|陸奥]][[盛岡藩]]預かりとなり、十太夫も[[高野山]]に追放された。なお、十太夫は[[島原の乱]]で黒田家に陣借りして鎮圧軍に従軍したが、さしたる戦功は挙げられず、黒田家復帰はならなかった。のち[[上方]]で死去したという。十太夫の孫・[[倉八宅兵衛]]に至り、ようやく再仕官を許されている。なお、この時に盛岡藩へ預かりとなった栗山大膳は、藩祖・黒田如水所用の[[兜]]も一緒に持参した。現在、もりおか歴史文化館に所蔵されている。
== 題材にした作品 == この事件を[[森鷗外]]が小説『[[栗山利章#黒田騒動を題材にした作品|栗山大膳]]』において、改易を危惧した大膳が黒田家を守るために尋問の場で訴えたとして脚色して描いている。十太夫は、島原の乱で一揆軍に加わり戦死したことになっている。
また、江戸時代中期には江戸[[中村座]]などにて黒田騒動を題材にした[[歌舞伎]]が数多く上演された。最初は[[瀬川如皐]]作、外題『[[御伽譚博多新織]]』。のちに[[河竹黙阿弥]]により狂言『[[黒白論織分博多]]』として加筆集約化された。また幕末には[[合巻]]としては最長編である『[[しらぬひ譚]]』も歌舞伎化され、こちらも大好評を博した。
{{DEFAULTSORT:くろたそうとう}} [[Category:福岡藩]] [[Category:福岡黒田家]] [[Category:福岡県の歴史]] [[Category:福岡市の歴史]] [[Category:江戸時代の事件]]

revision.comment: /* 題材にした作品 */ 追加。

'''黒田騒動'''（くろだそうどう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[福岡藩]]で発生した[[お家騒動]]。'''[[栗山大膳]]事件'''（くりやまだいぜんじけん）ともいう。[[伊達騒動]]、[[加賀騒動]]または[[仙石騒動]]とともに三大お家騒動と呼ばれる。他の御家騒動では処分時に死者が出ているが、黒田騒動ではお預けなどはあったものの、死者は出なかった。
== 経緯 == [[黒田長政]]は世継ぎ継承にあたり長男・[[黒田忠之|忠之]]の狭器と粗暴な性格を憂い、三男の[[黒田長興|長興]]に家督を譲ると決めて忠之に書状を送る。書状には2千石の田地で百姓をするか、1万両を与えるから[[関西]]で商人になるか、千石の知行で一寺建立して僧侶になるか、と非常に厳しいものであった。これに後見役の[[栗山利章|栗山大膳]]は、辱めを受けるのなら[[切腹]]をとの対応を忠之に勧める。そして600石以上2千石未満の藩士の嫡子たちを集め、長政に対して廃嫡を取りやめなければ全員切腹すると[[血判状]]をとった。この事態を重く見た長政は嘆願を受け入れ、大膳を後見役に頼んだ後に死去した。そこで大膳は忠之に諌書を送ったが、これが飲酒の心得や早寝早起きなど子供を諭すような内容だったため、忠之は大膳に対し立腹し、次第に距離を置くようになる。忠之は[[寛永]]元年（[[1624年]]）に藩主就任早々、忠之及びその側近と、[[筆頭家老]]であった大膳はじめ宿老達との間に軋轢を生じさせ、生前の長政が憂いていたとおりに御家騒動へと発展した。忠之は[[小姓]]から仕えていた[[倉八正俊|倉八十太夫]]（くらはち じゅうだゆう。名は正俊、または家頼）を側近として抱え、1万石の[[大身]]とした。そして十太夫に命じて豪華な大船「鳳凰丸」を建造。さらに200人の[[足軽]]を新規に雇い入れるなど、軍縮の時代にあってそれに逆行する暴政を行った。これにより遂に藩は幕府より咎めを受けるに至った。
大膳も寛永9年（[[1632年]]）6月、忠之が幕府転覆を狙っていると幕府に上訴した。藩側は「大膳は狂人である」との主張を行い、寛永10年（[[1633年]]）2月、将軍[[徳川家光]]が直々に裁いた結果、忠之の藩側の主張を認め、所領安堵の触れを出し、10年に及ぶ抗争に幕を閉じた。大膳は騒動の責を負って[[陸奥国|陸奥]][[盛岡藩]]預かりとなり、十太夫も[[高野山]]に追放された。なお、十太夫は[[島原の乱]]で黒田家に陣借りして鎮圧軍に従軍したが、さしたる戦功は挙げられず、黒田家復帰はならなかった。のち[[上方]]で死去したという。十太夫の孫・[[倉八宅兵衛]]に至り、ようやく再仕官を許されている。なお、この時に盛岡藩へ預かりとなった栗山大膳は、藩祖・黒田如水所用の[[兜]]も一緒に持参した。現在、もりおか歴史文化館に所蔵されている。
== 題材にした作品 == この事件を[[森鷗外]]が小説『[[栗山利章#黒田騒動を題材にした作品|栗山大膳]]』において、改易を危惧した大膳が黒田家を守るために尋問の場で訴えたとして脚色して描いている。十太夫は、島原の乱で一揆軍に加わり戦死したことになっている。[[滝口康彦]]の小説『主家滅ぶべし』では、利章は真面目で実直なものの、その忠義も独善的であるため主君と家中から疎まれてしまい、忠臣としての自身を保つために引き起こしたものとして描かれている。
また、江戸時代中期には江戸[[中村座]]などにて黒田騒動を題材にした[[歌舞伎]]が数多く上演された。最初は[[瀬川如皐]]作、外題『[[御伽譚博多新織]]』。のちに[[河竹黙阿弥]]により狂言『[[黒白論織分博多]]』として加筆集約化された。また幕末には[[合巻]]としては最長編である『[[しらぬひ譚]]』も歌舞伎化され、こちらも大好評を博した。

'''黒田騒動'''（くろだそうどう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[福岡藩]]で発生した[[お家騒動]]。'''[[栗山大膳]]事件'''（くりやまだいぜんじけん）ともいう。[[伊達騒動]]、[[加賀騒動]]または[[仙石騒動]]とともに三大お家騒動と呼ばれる。他の御家騒動では処分時に死者が出ているが、黒田騒動ではお預けなどはあったものの、死者は出なかった。

1956年には東映で映画『[[黒田騒動 (映画)|黒田騒動]]』が公開されている。監督は[[内田吐夢]]、主演は栗山大膳役は[[片岡千恵蔵]]。

{{出典の明記|date=2020-12}}
'''黒田騒動'''（くろだそうどう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[福岡藩]]で発生した[[お家騒動]]。'''[[栗山大膳]]事件'''（くりやまだいぜんじけん）ともいう。[[伊達騒動]]、[[加賀騒動]]または[[仙石騒動]]とともに三大お家騒動と呼ばれる。他の御家騒動では処分時に死者が出ているが、黒田騒動ではお預けなどはあったものの、死者は出なかった。
== 経緯 == [[黒田長政]]は世継ぎ継承にあたり長男・[[黒田忠之|忠之]]の狭器と粗暴な性格を憂い、三男の[[黒田長興|長興]]に家督を譲ると決めて忠之に書状を送る。書状には2千石の田地で百姓をするか、1万両を与えるから[[関西]]で商人になるか、千石の知行で一寺建立して僧侶になるか、と非常に厳しいものであった。これに後見役の[[栗山利章|栗山大膳]]は、辱めを受けるのなら[[切腹]]をとの対応を忠之に勧める。そして600石以上2千石未満の藩士の嫡子たちを集め、長政に対して廃嫡を取りやめなければ全員切腹すると[[血判状]]をとった。この事態を重く見た長政は嘆願を受け入れ、大膳を後見役に頼んだ後に死去した。そこで大膳は忠之に諌書を送ったが、これが飲酒の心得や早寝早起きなど子供を諭すような内容だったため、忠之は大膳に対し立腹し、次第に距離を置くようになる。忠之は[[寛永]]元年（[[1624年]]）に藩主就任早々、忠之及びその側近と、[[筆頭家老]]であった大膳はじめ宿老達との間に軋轢を生じさせ、生前の長政が憂いていたとおりに御家騒動へと発展した。忠之は[[小姓]]から仕えていた[[倉八正俊|倉八十太夫]]（くらはち じゅうだゆう。名は正俊、または家頼）を側近として抱え、1万石の[[大身]]とした。そして十太夫に命じて豪華な大船「鳳凰丸」を建造。さらに200人の[[足軽]]を新規に雇い入れるなど、軍縮の時代にあってそれに逆行する暴政を行った。これにより遂に藩は幕府より咎めを受けるに至った。
大膳も寛永9年（[[1632年]]）6月、忠之が幕府転覆を狙っていると幕府に上訴した。藩側は「大膳は狂人である」との主張を行い、寛永10年（[[1633年]]）2月、将軍[[徳川家光]]が直々に裁いた結果、忠之の藩側の主張を認め、所領安堵の触れを出し、10年に及ぶ抗争に幕を閉じた。大膳は騒動の責を負って[[陸奥国|陸奥]][[盛岡藩]]預かりとなり、十太夫も[[高野山]]に追放された。なお、十太夫は[[島原の乱]]で黒田家に陣借りして鎮圧軍に従軍したが、さしたる戦功は挙げられず、黒田家復帰はならなかった。のち[[上方]]で死去したという。十太夫の孫・[[倉八宅兵衛]]に至り、ようやく再仕官を許されている。なお、この時に盛岡藩へ預かりとなった栗山大膳は、藩祖・黒田如水所用の[[兜]]も一緒に持参した。現在、もりおか歴史文化館に所蔵されている。
== 題材にした作品 == 江戸時代中期には江戸[[中村座]]などにて黒田騒動を題材にした[[歌舞伎]]が数多く上演された。最初は[[瀬川如皐]]作、外題『[[御伽譚博多新織]]』。のちに[[河竹黙阿弥]]により狂言『[[黒白論織分博多]]』として加筆集約化された。また幕末には[[合巻]]としては最長編である『[[しらぬひ譚]]』も歌舞伎化され、こちらも大好評を博した。
また、この事件を[[森鷗外]]が小説『[[栗山利章#黒田騒動を題材にした作品|栗山大膳]]』において、改易を危惧した大膳が黒田家を守るために尋問の場で訴えたとして脚色して描いている。十太夫は、島原の乱で一揆軍に加わり戦死したことになっている。[[滝口康彦]]の小説『主家滅ぶべし』では、利章は真面目で実直なものの、その忠義も独善的であるため主君と家中から疎まれてしまい、忠臣としての自身を保つために引き起こしたものとして描かれている。
1956年には東映で映画『[[黒田騒動 (映画)|黒田騒動]]』が公開されている。監督は[[内田吐夢]]、主演の栗山大膳役は[[片岡千恵蔵]]。

1965年[[海音寺潮五郎]]が「列藩騒動録」新潮社を上梓、のち講談社で文庫化。

また、この事件を[[森鷗外]]が小説『[[栗山利章#黒田騒動を題材にした作品|栗山大膳]]』において、改易を危惧した大膳が黒田家を守るために尋問の場で訴えたとして脚色して描いている。十太夫は、島原の乱で一揆軍に加わり戦死したことになっている。[[滝口康彦]]の小説『主家滅ぶべし』では、利章は真面目で実直なものの、その忠義も独善的であるため主君と家中から疎まれてしまい、忠臣としての自身を保つために引き起こしたものとして描かれている。
1956年には東映で映画『[[黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)|黒田騒動]]』が公開されている。監督は[[内田吐夢]]、主演の栗山大膳役は[[片岡千恵蔵]]。
1965年[[海音寺潮五郎]]が「列藩騒動録」新潮社を上梓、のち講談社で文庫化。

{{出典の明記|date=2020-12}} {{Otheruses|江戸時代のお家騒動|映画|黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)}} '''黒田騒動'''（くろだそうどう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[福岡藩]]で発生した[[お家騒動]]。'''[[栗山大膳]]事件'''（くりやまだいぜんじけん）ともいう。[[伊達騒動]]、[[加賀騒動]]または[[仙石騒動]]とともに三大お家騒動と呼ばれる。他の御家騒動では処分時に死者が出ているが、黒田騒動ではお預けなどはあったものの、死者は出なかった。
== 経緯 == [[黒田長政]]は世継ぎ継承にあたり長男・[[黒田忠之|忠之]]の狭器と粗暴な性格を憂い、三男の[[黒田長興|長興]]に家督を譲ると決めて忠之に書状を送る。書状には2千石の田地で百姓をするか、1万両を与えるから[[関西]]で商人になるか、千石の知行で一寺建立して僧侶になるか、と非常に厳しいものであった。これに後見役の[[栗山利章|栗山大膳]]は、辱めを受けるのなら[[切腹]]をとの対応を忠之に勧める。そして600石以上2千石未満の藩士の嫡子たちを集め、長政に対して廃嫡を取りやめなければ全員切腹すると[[血判状]]をとった。この事態を重く見た長政は嘆願を受け入れ、大膳を後見役に頼んだ後に死去した。そこで大膳は忠之に諌書を送ったが、これが飲酒の心得や早寝早起きなど子供を諭すような内容だったため、忠之は大膳に対し立腹し、次第に距離を置くようになる。忠之は[[寛永]]元年（[[1624年]]）に藩主就任早々、忠之及びその側近と、[[筆頭家老]]であった大膳はじめ宿老達との間に軋轢を生じさせ、生前の長政が憂いていたとおりに御家騒動へと発展した。忠之は[[小姓]]から仕えていた[[倉八正俊|倉八十太夫]]（くらはち じゅうだゆう。名は正俊、または家頼）を側近として抱え、1万石の[[大身]]とした。そして十太夫に命じて豪華な大船「鳳凰丸」を建造。さらに200人の[[足軽]]を新規に雇い入れるなど、軍縮の時代にあってそれに逆行する暴政を行った。これにより遂に藩は幕府より咎めを受けるに至った。
大膳も寛永9年（[[1632年]]）6月、忠之が幕府転覆を狙っていると幕府に上訴した。藩側は「大膳は狂人である」との主張を行い、寛永10年（[[1633年]]）2月、将軍[[徳川家光]]が直々に裁いた結果、忠之の藩側の主張を認め、所領安堵の触れを出し、10年に及ぶ抗争に幕を閉じた。大膳は騒動の責を負って[[陸奥国|陸奥]][[盛岡藩]]預かりとなり、十太夫も[[高野山]]に追放された。なお、十太夫は[[島原の乱]]で黒田家に陣借りして鎮圧軍に従軍したが、さしたる戦功は挙げられず、黒田家復帰はならなかった。のち[[上方]]で死去したという。十太夫の孫・[[倉八宅兵衛]]に至り、ようやく再仕官を許されている。なお、この時に盛岡藩へ預かりとなった栗山大膳は、藩祖・黒田如水所用の[[兜]]も一緒に持参した。現在、もりおか歴史文化館に所蔵されている。
== 題材にした作品 == 江戸時代中期には江戸[[中村座]]などにて黒田騒動を題材にした[[歌舞伎]]が数多く上演された。最初は[[瀬川如皐]]作、外題『[[御伽譚博多新織]]』。のちに[[河竹黙阿弥]]により狂言『[[黒白論織分博多]]』として加筆集約化された。また幕末には[[合巻]]としては最長編である『[[しらぬひ譚]]』も歌舞伎化され、こちらも大好評を博した。
また、この事件を[[森鷗外]]が小説『[[栗山利章#黒田騒動を題材にした作品|栗山大膳]]』において、改易を危惧した大膳が黒田家を守るために尋問の場で訴えたとして脚色して描いている。十太夫は、島原の乱で一揆軍に加わり戦死したことになっている。[[滝口康彦]]の小説『主家滅ぶべし』では、利章は真面目で実直なものの、その忠義も独善的であるため主君と家中から疎まれてしまい、忠臣としての自身を保つために引き起こしたものとして描かれている。
1956年には東映で映画『[[黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)|黒田騒動]]』が公開されている。監督は[[内田吐夢]]、主演の栗山大膳役は[[片岡千恵蔵]]。
1965年[[海音寺潮五郎]]が「列藩騒動録」新潮社を上梓、のち講談社で文庫化。

{{出典の明記|date=2020-12}} {{Otheruses|江戸時代のお家騒動|映画|黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)}} '''黒田騒動'''（くろだそうどう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[福岡藩]]で発生した[[お家騒動]]。'''栗山大膳事件'''（くりやまだいぜんじけん）ともいう。[[伊達騒動]]、[[加賀騒動]]または[[仙石騒動]]とともに三大お家騒動と呼ばれる。他の御家騒動では処分時に死者が出ているが、黒田騒動ではお預けなどはあったものの、死者は出なかった。
== 経緯 == [[黒田長政]]は世継ぎ継承にあたり長男・[[黒田忠之|忠之]]の狭器と粗暴な性格を憂い、三男の[[黒田長興|長興]]に家督を譲ると決めて忠之に書状を送る。書状には2千石の田地で百姓をするか、1万両を与えるから[[関西]]で商人になるか、千石の知行で一寺建立して僧侶になるか、と非常に厳しいものであった。これに後見役の[[栗山利章|栗山大膳]]は、辱めを受けるのなら[[切腹]]をとの対応を忠之に勧める。そして600石以上2千石未満の藩士の嫡子たちを集め、長政に対して廃嫡を取りやめなければ全員切腹すると[[血判状]]をとった。この事態を重く見た長政は嘆願を受け入れ、大膳を後見役に頼んだ後に死去した。そこで大膳は忠之に諌書を送ったが、これが飲酒の心得や早寝早起きなど子供を諭すような内容だったため、忠之は大膳に対し立腹し、次第に距離を置くようになる。忠之は[[寛永]]元年（[[1624年]]）に藩主就任早々、忠之およびその側近と、[[筆頭家老]]であった大膳はじめ宿老達との間に軋轢を生じさせ、生前の長政が憂いていたとおりに御家騒動へと発展した。忠之は[[小姓]]から仕えていた[[倉八正俊|倉八十太夫]]（名は正俊、または家頼）を側近として抱え、1万石の[[大身]]とした。そして十太夫に命じて豪華な大船「鳳凰丸」を建造。さらに200人の[[足軽]]を新規に雇い入れるなど、軍縮の時代にあってそれに逆行する暴政を行った。これにより遂に藩は幕府より咎めを受けるに至った。
大膳も寛永9年（[[1632年]]）6月、忠之が幕府転覆を狙っていると幕府に上訴した。藩側は「大膳は狂人である」との主張を行い、寛永10年（[[1633年]]）2月、将軍[[徳川家光]]が直々に裁いた結果、忠之の藩側の主張を認め、所領安堵の触れを出し、10年におよぶ抗争に幕を閉じた。大膳は騒動の責を負って[[陸奥国|陸奥]][[盛岡藩]]預かりとなり、十太夫も[[高野山]]に追放された。なお、十太夫は[[島原の乱]]で黒田家に陣借りして鎮圧軍に従軍したが、さしたる戦功は挙げられず、黒田家復帰はならなかった。のち[[上方]]で死去したという。十太夫の孫・[[倉八宅兵衛]]に至り、ようやく再仕官を許されている。なお、この時に盛岡藩へ預かりとなった栗山大膳は、藩祖・黒田如水所用の[[兜]]も一緒に持参した。現在、もりおか歴史文化館に所蔵されている。
== 題材にした作品 == 江戸時代中期には江戸[[中村座]]などにて黒田騒動を題材にした[[歌舞伎]]が数多く上演された。最初は[[瀬川如皐]]作、外題『[[御伽譚博多新織]]』。のちに[[河竹黙阿弥]]により狂言『[[黒白論織分博多]]』として加筆集約化された。また幕末には[[合巻]]としては最長編である『[[しらぬひ譚]]』も歌舞伎化され、こちらも大好評を博した。
また、この事件を[[森鷗外]]が小説『[[栗山利章#黒田騒動を題材にした作品|栗山大膳]]』において、改易を危惧した大膳が黒田家を守るために尋問の場で訴えたとして脚色して描いている。十太夫は、島原の乱で一揆軍に加わり戦死したことになっている。[[滝口康彦]]の小説『主家滅ぶべし』では、利章は真面目で実直なものの、その忠義も独善的であるため主君と家中から疎まれてしまい、忠臣としての自身を保つために引き起こしたものとして描かれている。
1956年には東映で映画『[[黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)|黒田騒動]]』が公開されている。監督は[[内田吐夢]]、主演の栗山大膳役は[[片岡千恵蔵]]。
1965年[[海音寺潮五郎]]が「列藩騒動録」新潮社を上梓、のち講談社で文庫化。
1971年、[[山田風太郎]]が短編『妖剣林田左文』で黒田騒動へとつながる事件を描いている。

[[ja:%E9%BB%92%E7%94%B0%E9%A8%92%E5%8B%95]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[Category:Https://fa.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[fa:%DA%A9%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%AF%D8%A7 %D8%B3%D9%88%D8%AF%D9%88]]

[[ja:%E9%BB%92%E7%94%B0%E9%A8%92%E5%8B%95]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[Category:Https://fa.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[fa:%DA%A9%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%AF%D8%A7 %D8%B3%D9%88%D8%AF%D9%88]] == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an {{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|ja|お家騒動}} (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|ja|福岡藩}} during the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the {{ill|Date Disturbance|ja|伊達騒動}} and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
{{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|ja|黒田長政}}, fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, and Kurahachi was exiled to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous {{ill|kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}} plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多}}" by {{ill|Kawatake Mokuami|ja|河竹黙阿弥}}. The longest {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, {{ill|Mori Ōgai|ja|森鷗外}} fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)}}" directed by {{ill|Uchida Tomu|ja|内田吐夢}}, starring {{ill|Kataoka Chiezō|ja|片岡千恵蔵}} as Daizen.

[[ja:%E9%BB%92%E7%94%B0%E9%A8%92%E5%8B%95]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[Category:Https://fa.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[fa:%DA%A9%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%AF%D8%A7 %D8%B3%D9%88%D8%AF%D9%88]] == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an {{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|ja|お家騒動}} (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|ja|福岡藩}} during the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the {{ill|Date Disturbance|ja|伊達騒動}} and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
{{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|ja|黒田長政}}, fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, and Kurahachi was exiled to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous {{ill|kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}} plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多}}" by {{ill|Kawatake Mokuami|ja|河竹黙阿弥}}. The longest {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, {{ill|Mori Ōgai|ja|森鷗外}} fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)}}" directed by {{ill|Uchida Tomu|ja|内田吐夢}}, starring {{ill|Kataoka Chiezō|ja|片岡千恵蔵}} as Daizen.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Related Topics ==

[[ja:%E9%BB%92%E7%94%B0%E9%A8%92%E5%8B%95]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[fa:%DA%A9%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%AF%D8%A7 %D8%B3%D9%88%D8%AF%D9%88]] == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an {{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|ja|お家騒動}} (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|ja|福岡藩}} during the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the {{ill|Date Disturbance|ja|伊達騒動}} and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
{{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|ja|黒田長政}}, fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, and Kurahachi was exiled to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous {{ill|kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}} plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多}}" by {{ill|Kawatake Mokuami|ja|河竹黙阿弥}}. The longest {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, {{ill|Mori Ōgai|ja|森鷗外}} fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)}}" directed by {{ill|Uchida Tomu|ja|内田吐夢}}, starring {{ill|Kataoka Chiezō|ja|片岡千恵蔵}} as Daizen.

revision.text:
[[ja:%E9%BB%92%E7%94%B0%E9%A8%92%E5%8B%95]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[fa:%DA%A9%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%AF%D8%A7 %D8%B3%D9%88%D8%AF%D9%88]] == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an {{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|ja|お家騒動}} (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|ja|福岡藩}} during the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the {{ill|Date Disturbance|ja|伊達騒動}} and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
{{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|ja|黒田長政}}, fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, and Kurahachi was exiled to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous {{ill|kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}} plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多}}" by {{ill|Kawatake Mokuami|ja|河竹黙阿弥}}. The longest {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, {{ill|Mori Ōgai|ja|森鷗外}} fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)}}" directed by {{ill|Uchida Tomu|ja|内田吐夢}}, starring {{ill|Kataoka Chiezō|ja|片岡千恵蔵}} as Daizen.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}== See Also ==
* {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|ja|福岡藩}} * {{ill|Kuroda Clan of Fukuoka|ja|福岡黒田家}} * {{ill|History of Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県の歴史}} * {{ill|History of Fukuoka City|ja|福岡市の歴史}} * {{ill|Edo-period incidents|ja|江戸時代の事件}} * {{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|ja|お家騒動}} * {{ill|1632 in Japan|ja|1632年の日本}} * {{ill|1633 in Japan|ja|1633年の日本}}

In modern literature, {{ill|Mori Ōgai|ja|森鷗外}} fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)}}" directed by {{ill|Uchida Tomu|ja|内田吐夢}}, starring {{ill|Kataoka Chiezō|ja|片岡千恵蔵}} as Daizen.

[[ja:%E9%BB%92%E7%94%B0%E9%A8%92%E5%8B%95]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[en:Kuroda Incident]] [[fa:%DA%A9%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%AF%D8%A7 %D8%B3%D9%88%D8%AF%D9%88]] == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an {{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|ja|お家騒動}} (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|ja|福岡藩}} during the early {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the {{ill|Date Disturbance|ja|伊達騒動}} and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
{{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|ja|黒田長政}}, fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|ja|徳川家光}} personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in {{ill|Morioka Domain|ja|盛岡藩}}, and Kurahachi was exiled to {{ill|Mount Kōya|ja|高野山}}. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous {{ill|kabuki|ja|歌舞伎}} plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多}}" by {{ill|Kawatake Mokuami|ja|河竹黙阿弥}}. The longest {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, {{ill|Mori Ōgai|ja|森鷗外}} fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)}}" directed by {{ill|Uchida Tomu|ja|内田吐夢}}, starring {{ill|Kataoka Chiezō|ja|片岡千恵蔵}} as Daizen.
== References ==

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2020-12}} {{Otheruses|江戸時代のお家騒動|映画|黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)}} '''黒田騒動'''（くろだそうどう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[福岡藩]]で発生した[[お家騒動]]。'''栗山大膳事件'''（くりやまだいぜんじけん）ともいう。[[伊達騒動]]、[[加賀騒動]]または[[仙石騒動]]とともに三大お家騒動と呼ばれる。他の御家騒動では処分時に死者が出ているが、黒田騒動ではお預けなどはあったものの、死者は出なかった。
== 経緯 == [[黒田長政]]は世継ぎ継承にあたり長男・[[黒田忠之|忠之]]の狭器と粗暴な性格を憂い、三男の[[黒田長興|長興]]に家督を譲ると決めて忠之に書状を送る。書状には2千石の田地で百姓をするか、1万両を与えるから[[関西]]で商人になるか、千石の知行で一寺建立して僧侶になるか、と非常に厳しいものであった。これに後見役の[[栗山利章|栗山大膳]]は、辱めを受けるのなら[[切腹]]をとの対応を忠之に勧める。そして600石以上2千石未満の藩士の嫡子たちを集め、長政に対して廃嫡を取りやめなければ全員切腹すると[[血判状]]をとった。この事態を重く見た長政は嘆願を受け入れ、大膳を後見役に頼んだ後に死去した。そこで大膳は忠之に諌書を送ったが、これが飲酒の心得や早寝早起きなど子供を諭すような内容だったため、忠之は大膳に対し立腹し、次第に距離を置くようになる。忠之は[[寛永]]元年（[[1624年]]）に藩主就任早々、忠之およびその側近と、[[家老|筆頭家老]]であった大膳はじめ宿老達との間に軋轢を生じさせ、生前の長政が憂いていたとおりに御家騒動へと発展した。忠之は[[小姓]]から仕えていた[[倉八正俊|倉八十太夫]]（名は正俊、または家頼）を側近として抱え、1万石の[[大身]]とした。そして十太夫に命じて豪華な大船「鳳凰丸」を建造。さらに200人の[[足軽]]を新規に雇い入れるなど、軍縮の時代にあってそれに逆行する暴政を行った。これにより遂に藩は幕府より咎めを受けるに至った。
大膳も寛永9年（[[1632年]]）6月、忠之が幕府転覆を狙っていると幕府に上訴した。藩側は「大膳は狂人である」との主張を行い、寛永10年（[[1633年]]）2月、将軍[[徳川家光]]が直々に裁いた結果、忠之の藩側の主張を認め、所領安堵の触れを出し、10年におよぶ抗争に幕を閉じた。大膳は騒動の責を負って[[陸奥国|陸奥]][[盛岡藩]]預かりとなり、十太夫も[[高野山]]に追放された。なお、十太夫は[[島原の乱]]で黒田家に陣借りして鎮圧軍に従軍したが、さしたる戦功は挙げられず、黒田家復帰はならなかった。のち[[上方]]で死去したという。十太夫の孫・倉八宅兵衛に至り、ようやく再仕官を許されている。なお、この時に盛岡藩へ預かりとなった栗山大膳は、藩祖・[[黒田孝高|黒田如水]]所用の[[兜]]も一緒に持参した。現在、もりおか歴史文化館に所蔵されている。
== 題材にした作品 == 江戸時代中期には江戸[[中村座]]などにて黒田騒動を題材にした[[歌舞伎]]が数多く上演された。最初は[[瀬川如皐]]作、外題『[[御伽譚博多新織]]』。のちに[[河竹黙阿弥]]により狂言『[[黒白論織分博多]]』として加筆集約化された。また幕末には[[合巻]]としては最長編である『[[しらぬひ譚]]』も歌舞伎化され、こちらも大好評を博した。
また、この事件を[[森鷗外]]が小説『[[栗山利章#黒田騒動を題材にした作品|栗山大膳]]』において、改易を危惧した大膳が黒田家を守るために尋問の場で訴えたとして脚色して描いている。十太夫は、島原の乱で一揆軍に加わり戦死したことになっている。[[滝口康彦]]の小説『主家滅ぶべし』では、利章は真面目で実直なものの、その忠義も独善的であるため主君と家中から疎まれてしまい、忠臣としての自身を保つために引き起こしたものとして描かれている。
1956年には東映で映画『[[黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)|黒田騒動]]』が公開されている。監督は[[内田吐夢]]、主演の栗山大膳役は[[片岡千恵蔵]]。
1965年[[海音寺潮五郎]]が「列藩騒動録」新潮社を上梓、のち講談社で文庫化。
1971年、[[山田風太郎]]が短編『妖剣林田左文』で黒田騒動へとつながる事件を描いている。

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[黒田騒動]] to [[Kuroda Incident]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2020-12}} {{Otheruses|江戸時代のお家騒動|映画|黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)}} '''黒田騒動'''（くろだそうどう）は、[[江戸時代]]前期に[[福岡藩]]で発生した[[お家騒動]]。'''栗山大膳事件'''（くりやまだいぜんじけん）ともいう。[[伊達騒動]]、[[加賀騒動]]または[[仙石騒動]]とともに三大お家騒動と呼ばれる。他の御家騒動では処分時に死者が出ているが、黒田騒動ではお預けなどはあったものの、死者は出なかった。
== 経緯 == [[黒田長政]]は世継ぎ継承にあたり長男・[[黒田忠之|忠之]]の狭器と粗暴な性格を憂い、三男の[[黒田長興|長興]]に家督を譲ると決めて忠之に書状を送る。書状には2千石の田地で百姓をするか、1万両を与えるから[[関西]]で商人になるか、千石の知行で一寺建立して僧侶になるか、と非常に厳しいものであった。これに後見役の[[栗山利章|栗山大膳]]は、辱めを受けるのなら[[切腹]]をとの対応を忠之に勧める。そして600石以上2千石未満の藩士の嫡子たちを集め、長政に対して廃嫡を取りやめなければ全員切腹すると[[血判状]]をとった。この事態を重く見た長政は嘆願を受け入れ、大膳を後見役に頼んだ後に死去した。そこで大膳は忠之に諌書を送ったが、これが飲酒の心得や早寝早起きなど子供を諭すような内容だったため、忠之は大膳に対し立腹し、次第に距離を置くようになる。忠之は[[寛永]]元年（[[1624年]]）に藩主就任早々、忠之およびその側近と、[[家老|筆頭家老]]であった大膳はじめ宿老達との間に軋轢を生じさせ、生前の長政が憂いていたとおりに御家騒動へと発展した。忠之は[[小姓]]から仕えていた[[倉八正俊|倉八十太夫]]（名は正俊、または家頼）を側近として抱え、1万石の[[大身]]とした。そして十太夫に命じて豪華な大船「鳳凰丸」を建造。さらに200人の[[足軽]]を新規に雇い入れるなど、軍縮の時代にあってそれに逆行する暴政を行った。これにより遂に藩は幕府より咎めを受けるに至った。
大膳も寛永9年（[[1632年]]）6月、忠之が幕府転覆を狙っていると幕府に上訴した。藩側は「大膳は狂人である」との主張を行い、寛永10年（[[1633年]]）2月、将軍[[徳川家光]]が直々に裁いた結果、忠之の藩側の主張を認め、所領安堵の触れを出し、10年におよぶ抗争に幕を閉じた。大膳は騒動の責を負って[[陸奥国|陸奥]][[盛岡藩]]預かりとなり、十太夫も[[高野山]]に追放された。なお、十太夫は[[島原の乱]]で黒田家に陣借りして鎮圧軍に従軍したが、さしたる戦功は挙げられず、黒田家復帰はならなかった。のち[[上方]]で死去したという。十太夫の孫・倉八宅兵衛に至り、ようやく再仕官を許されている。なお、この時に盛岡藩へ預かりとなった栗山大膳は、藩祖・[[黒田孝高|黒田如水]]所用の[[兜]]も一緒に持参した。現在、もりおか歴史文化館に所蔵されている。
== 題材にした作品 == 江戸時代中期には江戸[[中村座]]などにて黒田騒動を題材にした[[歌舞伎]]が数多く上演された。最初は[[瀬川如皐]]作、外題『[[御伽譚博多新織]]』。のちに[[河竹黙阿弥]]により狂言『[[黒白論織分博多]]』として加筆集約化された。また幕末には[[合巻]]としては最長編である『[[しらぬひ譚]]』も歌舞伎化され、こちらも大好評を博した。
また、この事件を[[森鷗外]]が小説『[[栗山利章#黒田騒動を題材にした作品|栗山大膳]]』において、改易を危惧した大膳が黒田家を守るために尋問の場で訴えたとして脚色して描いている。十太夫は、島原の乱で一揆軍に加わり戦死したことになっている。[[滝口康彦]]の小説『主家滅ぶべし』では、利章は真面目で実直なものの、その忠義も独善的であるため主君と家中から疎まれてしまい、忠臣としての自身を保つために引き起こしたものとして描かれている。
1956年には東映で映画『[[黒田騒動 (1956年の映画)|黒田騒動]]』が公開されている。監督は[[内田吐夢]]、主演の栗山大膳役は[[片岡千恵蔵]]。
1965年[[海音寺潮五郎]]が「列藩騒動録」新潮社を上梓、のち講談社で文庫化。
1971年、[[山田風太郎]]が短編『妖剣林田左文』で黒田騒動へとつながる事件を描いている。

revision.text:
== Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|qq=}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|qq=}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|qq=}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|qq=}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== See Also ==
* [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] * {{ill|Kuroda Clan of Fukuoka|ja|福岡黒田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Clan of Fukuoka|qq=}} * {{ill|History of Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県の歴史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}} * {{ill|History of Fukuoka City|ja|福岡市の歴史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Fukuoka City|qq=}} * {{ill|Edo-period incidents|ja|江戸時代の事件|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo-period incidents|qq=}} * [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] * {{ill|1632 in Japan|ja|1632nd year の日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1632 in Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|1633 in Japan|ja|1633rd year の日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1633 in Japan|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|黒田騒動|version=98788090|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

revision.text:
== Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|qq=}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|qq=}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|qq=}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|qq=}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

== Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|qq=}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|qq=}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|qq=}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|qq=}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|qq=}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|qq=}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|qq=}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=}}", also dramatized the event.

The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

revision.text:
== Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|qq=}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|qq=}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|qq=}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|qq=}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== See Also ==
* [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] * {{ill|Kuroda Clan of Fukuoka|ja|福岡黒田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Clan of Fukuoka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|History of Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県の歴史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|History of Fukuoka City|ja|福岡市の歴史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Fukuoka City|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Edo-period incidents|ja|江戸時代の事件|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo-period incidents|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] * {{ill|1632 in Japan|ja|1632nd year の日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1632 in Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|1633 in Japan|ja|1633rd year の日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1633 in Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

== Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|qq=}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|qq=}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|qq=}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|qq=}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.

In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|qq=}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|qq=}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|qq=}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|qq=}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== See Also ==
* [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] * {{ill|Kuroda Clan of Fukuoka|ja|福岡黒田家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Clan of Fukuoka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|History of Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県の歴史|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|History of Fukuoka City|ja|福岡の Municipal History|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Fukuoka City|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Edo-period incidents|ja|江戸時代の事件|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo-period incidents|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] * {{ill|1632 in Japan|ja|1632nd year の日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1632 in Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|1633 in Japan|ja|1633rd year の日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1633 in Japan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{translated page|ja|黒田騒動|version=98788090|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:History of Fukuoka Prefecture]]
[[Category:1632 in Japan]] [[Category:1633 in Japan]]
[[Category:Completion tier 1]]
}}

Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.

[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|qq=}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|qq=}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|qq=}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q109357370]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|qq=|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|qq=|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|qq=|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|qq=|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Totaifu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=|1=Segawa Jokō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
