[[fa:%DA%A9%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%AF%D8%A7 %D8%B3%D9%88%D8%AF%D9%88]] [[Category:1633 in Japan]] [[Category:O-Ie Sōdō]] [[Category:Incidents of the Edo Period]] [[Category:Municipal History of Fukuoka City]] [[Category:Fukuoka Domain]] [[Category:Fukuoka Kuroda Family]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:کورودا سودو]] [[ja:黒田騒動]] {{wikidata link|Q109357370}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q109357370]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|qq=|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|qq=|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|qq=|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|qq=|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Totaifu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|qq=|1=Segawa Jokō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|qq=|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

[[fa:%DA%A9%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%AF%D8%A7 %D8%B3%D9%88%D8%AF%D9%88]] [[Category:1633 in Japan]] [[Category:O-Ie Sōdō]] [[Category:Incidents of the Edo Period]] [[Category:Municipal History of Fukuoka City]] [[Category:Fukuoka Domain]] [[Category:Fukuoka Kuroda Family]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:کورودا سودو]] [[ja:黒田騒動]] {{wikidata link|Q109357370}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q109357370]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Totaifu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|1=Segawa Jokō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q109357370]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Totaifu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|1=Segawa Jokō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

[[fa:%DA%A9%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%88%D8%AF%D8%A7 %D8%B3%D9%88%D8%AF%D9%88]] [[Category:1633 in Japan]] [[Category:O-Ie Sōdō]] [[Category:Incidents of the Edo Period]] [[Category:Municipal History of Fukuoka City]] [[Category:Fukuoka Domain]] [[Category:Fukuoka Kuroda Family]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fa:کورودا سودو]] [[ja:黒田騒動]] {{wikidata link|Q109357370}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q109357370]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Totaifu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|1=Segawa Jokō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== See Also ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q109357370]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Totaifu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|1=Segawa Jokō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== See Also ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q109357370]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Totaifu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|1=Segawa Jokō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q109357370]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an [[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]] (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the [[:en:Fukuoka Domain|Fukuoka Domain]] during the early [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the [[:en:Date Sōdō|Date Disturbance]] and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
[[:en:Kuroda Nagamasa|Kuroda Nagamasa]], fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Totaifu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in [[:en:Morioka Domain|Morioka Domain]], and Kurahachi was exiled to [[:en:Mount Kōya|Mount Kōya]]. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous [[:en:Kabuki|kabuki]] plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|1=Segawa Jokō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by [[:en:Kawatake Mokuami|Kawatake Mokuami]]. The longest [[:en:Gōkan|gōkan]] novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, [[:en:Mori Ōgai|Mori Ōgai]] fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by [[:en:Tomu Uchida|Uchida Tomu]], starring [[:en:Chiezō Kataoka|Kataoka Chiezō]] as Daizen.

By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q109357370]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:19, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an {{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō}} (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|WD=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain}} during the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the {{ill|Date Sōdō|en|Date Sōdō|WD=Q1172302|lt=Date Disturbance}} and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
{{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|en|Kuroda Nagamasa|WD=Q950496|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa}}, fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Totaifu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in {{ill|Morioka Domain|en|Morioka Domain|WD=Q908854|lt=Morioka Domain}}, and Kurahachi was exiled to {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}}. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=kabuki}} plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|1=Segawa Jokō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Kawatake Mokuami|en|Kawatake Mokuami|WD=Q1737345|lt=Kawatake Mokuami}}. The longest {{ill|Gōkan|en|Gōkan|WD=Q11412438|lt=gōkan}} novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, {{ill|Mori Ōgai|en|Mori Ōgai|WD=Q356960|lt=Mori Ōgai}} fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by {{ill|Tomu Uchida|en|Tomu Uchida|WD=Q2742512|lt=Uchida Tomu}}, starring {{ill|Chiezō Kataoka|en|Chiezō Kataoka|WD=Q2606691|lt=Kataoka Chiezō}} as Daizen.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== See Also ==

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]]
== Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an {{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō}} (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|WD=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain}} during the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the {{ill|Date Sōdō|en|Date Sōdō|WD=Q1172302|lt=Date Disturbance}} and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
{{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|en|Kuroda Nagamasa|WD=Q950496|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa}}, fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Totaifu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in {{ill|Morioka Domain|en|Morioka Domain|WD=Q908854|lt=Morioka Domain}}, and Kurahachi was exiled to {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya}}. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=kabuki}} plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|1=Segawa Jokō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Kawatake Mokuami|en|Kawatake Mokuami|WD=Q1737345|lt=Kawatake Mokuami}}. The longest {{ill|Gōkan|en|Gōkan|WD=Q11412438|lt=gōkan}} novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, {{ill|Mori Ōgai|en|Mori Ōgai|WD=Q356960|lt=Mori Ōgai}} fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by {{ill|Tomu Uchida|en|Tomu Uchida|WD=Q2742512|lt=Uchida Tomu}}, starring {{ill|Chiezō Kataoka|en|Chiezō Kataoka|WD=Q2606691|lt=Kataoka Chiezō}} as Daizen.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== See Also ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:کورودا سودو]][[ja:黒田騒動]]
== Kuroda Disturbance (黒田騒動) ==
The '''Kuroda Disturbance''' (黒田騒動, Kuroda Sōdō), also known as the '''Kuriyama Daizen Incident''' (栗山大膳事件), was an {{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō|1=O-Ie Sōdō}} (internal clan conflict) that occurred in the {{ill|Fukuoka Domain|en|Fukuoka Domain|WD=Q208766|lt=Fukuoka Domain|1=Fukuoka Domain}} during the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. It is considered one of the three major clan disputes alongside the {{ill|Date Sōdō|en|Date Sōdō|WD=Q1172302|lt=Date Disturbance|1=Date Sōdō}} and the {{ill|Kaga Disturbance|ja|加賀騒動|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaga Disturbance|1=Kaga Sōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11399752}}. Unlike other conflicts of this nature, the Kuroda Disturbance resulted in no fatalities, though punishments such as confinement were imposed.
== Background ==
{{ill|Kuroda Nagamasa|en|Kuroda Nagamasa|WD=Q950496|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa|1=Kuroda Nagamasa}}, fearing the reckless nature of his eldest son {{ill|Kuroda Tadayuki|ja|黒田忠之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Tadayuki|1=Tadayuki Kuroda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678600}}, decided to pass leadership of the clan to his third son, {{ill|Kuroda Nagaoki|ja|黒田長興|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Nagaoki|1=Kuroda Nagaoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678708}}. He sent a stern letter to Tadayuki, offering harsh choices: to become a farmer with 2,000 koku of land, a merchant in Kansai with 10,000 ryō, or a monk with a stipend of 1,000 koku.
Tadayuki's guardian, {{ill|Kuriyama Toshiaki|ja|栗山利章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Toshiaki|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}} (known as Daizen), advised him to commit seppuku if he felt dishonored. Kuriyama gathered the heirs of samurai holding between 600 and 2,000 koku, securing a blood oath to commit seppuku unless the disinheritance was revoked. This compelled Nagamasa to relent, appointing Daizen as Tadayuki's guardian before his death.
However, tensions grew between Daizen and Tadayuki, especially after Daizen sent a letter with paternal advice that Tadayuki found patronizing. Upon becoming lord in 1624, Tadayuki favored {{ill|Kurahachi Totaifu|ja|倉八十太夫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurahachi Totaifu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倉八正俊|1=Kurahachi Masatoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109288179}} as his close advisor, granting him a large stipend and commissioning extravagant projects, such as building the opulent ship "Hōōmaru" and hiring 200 new foot soldiers—actions viewed as reckless during an era of military downsizing.
== The Conflict ==
By 1632, Daizen accused Tadayuki of plotting against the Tokugawa shogunate. The domain claimed Daizen was mentally unstable. In 1633, {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu|1=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} personally adjudicated the case, siding with Tadayuki and affirming the clan's holdings, thus ending a decade-long conflict.
Daizen was placed under house arrest in {{ill|Morioka Domain|en|Morioka Domain|WD=Q908854|lt=Morioka Domain|1=Morioka Domain}}, and Kurahachi was exiled to {{ill|Mount Kōya|en|Mount Kōya|WD=Q535065|lt=Mount Kōya|1=Mount Kōya}}. Kurahachi later died in the Kansai region, with his family only regaining samurai status generations later.
== Cultural Depictions ==
The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=kabuki|1=Kabuki}} plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|1=Q11566464|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Kawatake Mokuami|en|Kawatake Mokuami|WD=Q1737345|lt=Kawatake Mokuami|1=Kawatake Mokuami}}. The longest {{ill|Gōkan|en|Gōkan|WD=Q11412438|lt=gōkan|1=Gōkan}} novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, {{ill|Mori Ōgai|en|Mori Ōgai|WD=Q356960|lt=Mori Ōgai|1=Mori Ōgai}} fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by {{ill|Tomu Uchida|en|Tomu Uchida|WD=Q2742512|lt=Uchida Tomu|1=Tomu Uchida}}, starring {{ill|Chiezō Kataoka|en|Chiezō Kataoka|WD=Q2606691|lt=Kataoka Chiezō|1=Chiezō Kataoka}} as Daizen.

The Kuroda Disturbance inspired numerous {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|qid=Q199701|lt=kabuki|1=Kabuki}} plays during the Edo period, including "{{ill|Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja|御伽譚博多新織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otogibanashi Hakata Ori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Segawa Jokō|ja|瀬川如皐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Segawa Jokō|1=Q11566464|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11566464}} and "{{ill|Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja|黒白論織分博多|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuhaku Oriwakemono Hakata|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Kawatake Mokuami|en|Kawatake Mokuami|qid=Q1737345|lt=Kawatake Mokuami|1=Kawatake Mokuami}}. The longest {{ill|Gōkan|en|Gōkan|qid=Q11412438|lt=gōkan|1=Gōkan}} novel, "{{ill|Shiranui Monogatari|ja|しらぬひ譚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiranui Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}", also dramatized the event.
In modern literature, {{ill|Mori Ōgai|en|Mori Ōgai|qid=Q356960|lt=Mori Ōgai|1=Mori Ōgai}} fictionalized the incident in "{{ill|Kuriyama Daizen|ja|栗山大膳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama Daizen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 栗山利章|1=Kuriyama Toshiaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11535908}}", portraying Daizen as a tragic figure striving to protect the Kuroda clan. {{ill|Takiguchi Yasuhiko|ja|滝口康彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takiguchi Yasuhiko|1=Yasuhiko Takiguchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11565085}}'s "Shuka Horobubeshi" presents Daizen as a rigid, self-righteous loyalist.
The story was adapted into the 1956 film "{{ill|Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja|黒田騒動 (1956th year の映画)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Disturbance (1956 film)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" directed by {{ill|Tomu Uchida|en|Tomu Uchida|qid=Q2742512|lt=Uchida Tomu|1=Tomu Uchida}}, starring {{ill|Chiezō Kataoka|en|Chiezō Kataoka|qid=Q2606691|lt=Kataoka Chiezō|1=Chiezō Kataoka}} as Daizen.
